feed_card.one_line: An unusually high or low result is usually followed by a more ordinary one, because luck rarely repeats.
feed_card.teaser_visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' opacity='0.5'><line x1='60' y1='240' x2='360' y2='240'/><line x1='60' y1='240' x2='60' y2='40'/></g><line x1='60' y1='240' x2='360' y2='40' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.8' stroke-dasharray='6 5' stroke-linecap='round' opacity='0.45'/><line x1='60' y1='200' x2='360' y2='80' stroke='#a78bfa' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><g fill='currentColor' opacity='0.55'><circle cx='110' cy='196' r='3.5'/><circle cx='150' cy='178' r='3.5'/><circle cx='195' cy='158' r='3.5'/><circle cx='235' cy='132' r='3.5'/><circle cx='280' cy='112' r='3.5'/></g></svg>
teasers: Why punishment seems to beat praise

sections.content: When something is measured at an unusually high or low point, the next measurement of the same thing tends to land closer to the average. Part of any extreme result is luck, and luck does not carry over.

A sales rep closes a record number of deals in March, the best month anyone on the team has seen. Management promotes her and raises her target. April comes in lower. The natural reading is that success went to her head, or the new pressure hurt. The duller reading is closer to the truth. March combined real ability with a run of warm leads, a competitor who stumbled, and a few clients who happened to be ready to buy that week. The ability carried into April. The lucky run did not. Strip out the luck and what remains is closer to ordinary, which is roughly where April landed. Nothing went wrong in April. Something unusually right happened in March, and unusual does not repeat on schedule.

content.image_caption: Each dot pairs a first and second measurement of the same thing. The dashed line is what pure skill would draw, where the second result equals the first. The violet line is what actually happens: it tilts flatter, so a high first score predicts a second score pulled back toward the average.

content.title: Every result mixes signal and luck
content.body: Any measured performance has two parts. One is stable: real skill, true quality, the underlying rate. The other is transient: luck, timing, mood, measurement error, the conditions of that one day. The stable part tends to repeat. The transient part does not.

content.title: Extremes need both parts aligned
content.body: A record high happens when the stable part is strong and the luck runs favorable at the same moment. Both pushing the same way at once is uncommon, which is why records are rare. The mirror image holds for a record low, where a weak underlying rate meets a bad draw of luck.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='70' y1='240' x2='360' y2='240' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' opacity='0.5'/><rect x='110' y='150' width='70' height='90' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='110' y='82' width='70' height='68' fill='#a78bfa' fill-opacity='0.18' stroke='#a78bfa' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linejoin='round'/><rect x='250' y='150' width='70' height='90' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='250' y='128' width='70' height='22' fill='#a78bfa' fill-opacity='0.18' stroke='#a78bfa' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linejoin='round'/><path d='M188 92 Q225 100 248 124' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.8' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-dasharray='4 4' opacity='0.5'/><path d='M248 124 l1 -9 m-1 9 l-9 -1' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='1.8' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.5'/><g font-family='system-ui, sans-serif' text-anchor='middle'><text x='145' y='200' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' font-weight='600'>skill</text><text x='285' y='200' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' font-weight='600'>skill</text><text x='145' y='120' fill='#a78bfa' font-size='11' font-weight='700'>luck</text><text x='285' y='143' fill='#a78bfa' font-size='10' font-weight='700'>luck</text><text x='145' y='258' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75'>Record day</text><text x='285' y='258' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' font-weight='600' opacity='0.75'>Next day</text></g></svg>

content.title: Luck resets on the next measurement
content.body: Measure the same thing again and the stable part is still there. The favorable luck is gone, replaced by a fresh draw that is, on average, ordinary. The second result keeps the skill but loses the tailwind.

content.title: The result drifts back toward average
content.body: With only the stable part and an ordinary draw of luck, the second measurement settles closer to the mean than the first. The more the first result owed to luck, the larger the drop. This drift needs no cause beyond arithmetic. Nothing has to act on the performer between the two measurements.

content.title: Weak correlation means strong regression
content.body: How far a result regresses depends on how much of it was luck. When two measurements of the same thing track each other tightly, the measure is mostly signal and there is little regression. When they track loosely, the measure is mostly noise and the second result falls almost all the way back to the average.

In standardized terms, the effect has an exact size. Write each measurement as the number of standard deviations it sits from the average. The best prediction for the second standardized score is the first standardized score multiplied by the correlation between the two measurements. Since that correlation is at most 1, the prediction always sits closer to zero, which is the mean. A correlation of 1 is pure signal and gives no regression. A correlation of 0 is pure noise and gives full regression to the average. A correlation of 0.5 means a first score two standard deviations above average predicts a second score only one standard deviation above average.

notation_legend.meaning: the first measurement, in standard deviations from the mean
the predicted second measurement, in standard deviations from the mean
the correlation between the two measurements, from 0 to 1

content.title: The sophomore slump is mostly arithmetic
content.body: An athlete wins Rookie of the Year, then disappoints in the second season, and commentators reach for reasons: complacency, pressure, opponents catching on. The rookie award goes to whoever combined real talent with a season where the breaks fell their way. The talent stays. The breaks scatter. A second season nearer the player's true level reads as a decline only against an inflated baseline. The same logic sits behind the Sports Illustrated cover effect, where featured athletes often fade afterward, because the cover finds them at a peak.

content.title: Speed cameras take credit for chance
content.body: Authorities install speed cameras where serious crashes recently spiked. Crashes then fall, and the camera is declared a success. Some of that fall is real deterrence. A large part is regression: a site gets chosen precisely because it had an unusually bad year, and unusually bad years are followed by ordinary ones whether or not a camera arrives. Road safety studies that ignore this have overstated camera benefits by wide margins, which is why careful evaluations compare against control sites that were equally bad and left untreated.

content.title: The second doctor visit looks better on its own
content.body: People book a doctor when symptoms are at their worst, because that is what drives them to call. Many conditions then ease regardless of what the doctor does, since the visit was timed to a low point that was always going to lift. The patient credits the new medicine and the doctor credits the treatment, when both may be reading a recovery that regression would have delivered anyway. This is why a serious trial needs a control group that is equally unwell and untreated.

content.title: Flight instructors learned the wrong lesson
content.body: Israeli air force instructors noticed that praising a cadet after an excellent maneuver was followed by a worse one, while shouting at a cadet after a poor maneuver was followed by improvement. They concluded that criticism works and praise backfires, and trained that way for years. The pattern was regression. An excellent maneuver is a high outlier whose next attempt lands closer to average, and a poor one is a low outlier whose next attempt lands better. The instructors were rewarding and punishing chance, then misreading the rebound as proof.

Reach for regression to the mean whenever you are about to draw a lesson from a before-and-after comparison. Ask first why this case landed in front of you. If you picked it because it was a high or a low point, a record quarter, a worst-ever month, a patient at their sickest, then expect the next measurement to move toward the average on its own, before you credit anything you did. The harder you selected on the extreme, the more free movement you should expect. To separate a real effect from this drift, you need a comparison: a group that was equally extreme and left untreated. If your treated group improves no more than that control, the change was regression. If it beats the control, you have found something real. When no control exists, at least ask whether the size of the change is larger than chance alone would produce.
checklist: Was this case chosen because it was a high or a low point?
Does the measure carry luck, timing, mood, or noise?
Is there a control that was equally extreme but left alone?
Did the change beat what regression alone would produce?
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><rect x='20' y='120' width='105' height='62' rx='10' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.8'/><rect x='148' y='120' width='105' height='62' rx='10' fill='#a78bfa' fill-opacity='0.12' stroke='#a78bfa' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linejoin='round'/><rect x='276' y='120' width='105' height='62' rx='10' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.8'/><g stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round' opacity='0.55'><path d='M125 151 h23'/><path d='M148 151 l-11 -6 m11 6 l-11 6'/><path d='M253 151 h23'/><path d='M276 151 l-11 -6 m11 6 l-11 6'/></g><g font-family='system-ui, sans-serif' text-anchor='middle'><text x='72' y='147' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' font-weight='600' opacity='0.85'>Picked for</text><text x='72' y='162' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' font-weight='600' opacity='0.85'>an extreme?</text><text x='200' y='147' fill='#a78bfa' font-size='11' font-weight='700'>Expect drift</text><text x='200' y='162' fill='#a78bfa' font-size='11' font-weight='700'>to the mean</text><text x='328' y='147' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' font-weight='600' opacity='0.85'>Beat a fair</text><text x='328' y='162' fill='currentColor' font-size='11' font-weight='600' opacity='0.85'>control?</text></g></svg>

sections.content: Regression to the mean is a property of selection plus noise, not a force, and treating it as a force creates its own errors. The most common is over-diagnosis: waving away a genuine effect as 'just regression' when a proper control shows the treated group improved more than chance would deliver. The opposite error is to forget the relevant average. A result regresses toward the mean of its own group, not toward some universal middle, so a consistently elite performer regresses toward an elite average, not toward mediocrity. Regression also says nothing certain about a single next case. It describes the tendency across many such cases, and any one performer can still post a second extreme. When a measure is almost pure signal, with little luck in it, there is barely any regression to invoke. In 1933 Horace Secrist filled a book with charts of firms drifting toward 'mediocrity' and concluded that competition erodes excellence. The statistician Harold Hotelling showed he had measured nothing but regression to the mean dressed up as an economic law.

content.body: When you single out a case because it stood out, expect its next showing to be tamer. The standout was part signal and part luck, and luck does not appear on cue twice. We are quick to read cause into these returns to normal, a manager who fixed things or a remedy that worked, when often we are watching arithmetic.
content.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' fill='none'><line x1='70' y1='180' x2='330' y2='180' stroke='currentColor' stroke-width='2' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-dasharray='5 5' opacity='0.4'/><text x='70' y='200' fill='currentColor' font-family='system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='11' font-weight='600' opacity='0.6'>average</text><circle cx='250' cy='80' r='6' fill='#a78bfa'/><path d='M250 90 Q300 135 256 172' stroke='#a78bfa' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round'/><path d='M256 172 l9 -3 m-9 3 l3 -9' stroke='#a78bfa' stroke-width='2.5' stroke-linecap='round' stroke-linejoin='round'/><text x='250' y='66' fill='#a78bfa' font-family='system-ui, sans-serif' font-size='11' font-weight='700' text-anchor='middle'>standout</text></svg>

content.question: A teacher screams at the class after their worst quiz of the year, and the next quiz scores improve. What is the most likely explanation?
options: Fear is an effective teaching method
The worst quiz was a low outlier, so scores were likely to rise on their own
The students studied harder out of resentment
The second quiz was easier
content.explanation: A worst-ever quiz is a low extreme, and extremes are followed by results nearer the average regardless of what the teacher does. To know whether the shouting helped, you would need a comparable class that scored just as badly and was not shouted at.

content.question: A supplement is advertised with glowing testimonials from people who started taking it when they felt at their absolute worst. Why is this evidence weak?
options: Testimonials are always fabricated
People who feel their worst tend to improve afterward anyway, with or without the supplement
The sample is too small to matter
The supplement has not been patented
content.explanation: Starting 'at their worst' selects a low point, and low points are followed by improvement on their own. The testimonials may be honest and still tell you nothing about the supplement, because regression predicts the same recovery without it.

content.question: In which case would you expect the LEAST regression to the mean between two measurements?
options: A basketball player's points across two different games
A person's height measured twice with a good tape measure
A restaurant's busiest single night and the night after
A stock's best trading day and the following day
content.explanation: Height measured carefully is almost pure signal, so the two measurements correlate near 1 and barely regress. The others all carry heavy day-to-day luck, which is exactly what produces large regression.

content.question: An investor picks the single best-performing fund of the past year and expects it to lead again next year. What does regression to the mean predict?
options: The fund will almost certainly lead again
The fund will likely perform closer to the average next year
The fund will become the worst performer
The fund's manager will quit
content.explanation: Topping the table in one year usually combines real skill with a favorable run. The skill may persist, but the lucky run is unlikely to repeat, so the expected next-year result sits closer to the pack, not at the top and not at the bottom.

content.question: Why does a clinical trial need a control group even when patients on the new drug clearly get better?
options: To make the study look more rigorous to reviewers
Because patients enrolled at their sickest would tend to improve anyway, and the control measures that baseline drift
To double the sample size
Because drugs only work in comparison to other drugs
content.explanation: Patients often enter a trial at a low point, so some recovery is regression, not the drug. A control group equally sick at the start shows how much improvement happens without treatment, so any extra gain in the treated group can be credited to the drug.

content.question: What is the underlying reason an extreme result tends to be followed by a milder one?
options: A natural force pulls values back toward the center
Part of the extreme came from luck that does not persist into the next measurement
The population average shifts between measurements
Measuring instruments grow more accurate over time
content.explanation: There is no pulling force. An extreme reading usually included a lucky push on top of the stable signal, and on the next measurement that push is replaced by an ordinary draw, leaving a result nearer the average.

content.question: A manager dismisses a training program's measured gains by saying 'that is just regression to the mean.' When is the manager wrong?
options: Whenever a before-and-after gain appears, regression fully explains it
When a matched group that was equally weak and skipped the training improved much less than the trained group
The manager is never wrong, since all gains are regression
When the training was expensive
content.explanation: Regression is the right suspicion, not the final verdict. If a control group that started equally weak improved far less, the trained group's extra gain is a real effect that regression cannot account for.

content.body: Francis Galton named the effect in 1886, in a paper titled 'Regression towards Mediocrity in Hereditary Stature.' He had gathered the heights of more than nine hundred adult children alongside their parents and noticed a pattern that puzzled him: tall parents had children taller than average, but shorter than the parents themselves, and short parents had children below average yet taller than themselves. Each generation leaned back toward the population's middle. Galton first called this 'reversion,' then settled on 'regression,' and the word later attached itself to the whole statistical method of fitting a line through data. The mathematics that explains it was sharpened by his student Karl Pearson into the correlation coefficient. Galton also coined the word 'eugenics' and pursued the heredity studies in its service, a use of the same data that the science has long since rejected.

key_thinkers.name: Francis Galton
key_thinkers.role: Named and first described the effect
key_thinkers.one_line: Victorian polymath who measured heredity, coined the term 'regression,' and also founded eugenics.

key_thinkers.name: Karl Pearson
key_thinkers.one_line: Galton's student, who turned the idea into the correlation coefficient and much of modern statistics.

content.distinction: The gambler's fallacy expects independent events to self-correct, as if a coin that landed heads five times now 'owes' tails. Regression involves no such force and no memory between events. It describes how a selected extreme, made partly of luck, tends to look milder on remeasurement.
Survivorship bias is about which cases are visible at all, since the failures drop out of sight. Regression is about how an already-visible extreme case behaves on the next measurement.

Financial mean reversion is an economic claim that prices tend back toward a fundamental value, a statement about a real process that may or may not hold. Statistical regression to the mean is a property of noisy measurement and selection, and it appears even when nothing in the world pulls anything back.

content.label: Galton F. Regression towards Mediocrity in Hereditary Stature. Journal of the Anthropological Institute 1886;15:246-263
Barnett AG, van der Pols JC, Dobson AJ. Regression to the mean: what it is and how to deal with it. International Journal of Epidemiology 2005;34(1):215-220
Kahneman D. Thinking, Fast and Slow. Farrar, Straus and Giroux, 2011 (Chapter 17, Regression to the Mean)
Hotelling H. Review of The Triumph of Mediocrity in Business by Horace Secrist. Journal of the American Statistical Association 1933;28(184):463-465
